perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it will not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to any person, firm or business, except to the extent necessary for negotiations, discussions, and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of the other party and any purpose the other party may hereafter authorize in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it will endeavor not to make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to any person, firm or business, except to the extent necessary for negotiations, discussions, and consultations with personnel or authorized representatives of the other party and any purpose the other party may hereafter authorize in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agrees that it will not" with "agrees that it will endeavor not to" weakens the obligation, introducing ambiguity about the required level of commitment. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, not a guarantee, making enforcement difficult. This weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets. While a weakened NDA doesn't directly violate the EEA, by making the non-disclosure obligation ambiguous, it creates a loophole that could impede the ability of a party to prove misappropriation in court, thus indirectly undermining the intent and effectiveness of the EEA's protections against trade secret theft. The ambiguity makes it harder to pursue legal action under the EEA if confidential information is disclosed.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original text's firm commitment to non-disclosure is weakened by the changed text, which uses 'endeavor not to,' thus creating ambiguity. While the Economic Espionage Act itself is not directly violated, the Law Explanation states that by introducing ambiguity, 'it creates a loophole that could impede the ability of a party to prove misappropriation in court, thus indirectly undermining the intent and effectiveness of the EEA's protections against trade secret theft.' This falls under Ambiguities Legal Contradiction, where the ambiguous obligation makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law, potentially leaving legal obligations open to dispute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions '18 USC 1832: Theft of trade secrets' which the change indirectly undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall disclose Confidential Information of the other party only to those of its employees, consultants, advisors and investors who need to know such information and who have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantially similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it should attempt to disclose Confidential Information of the other party only to those of its employees, consultants, advisors and investors who may need to know such information and who have, to the best of their understanding, agreed to be bound by terms and conditions that are somewhat similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall disclose only to those who need to know' to 'should attempt to disclose only to those who may need to know' introduces ambiguity around the obligation and necessity. Changing 'substantially similar' to 'somewhat similar' further weakens the requirement for confidentiality agreements with employees/consultants.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets. The original text has strong requirements, limiting disclosure to those with a specific need to know and requiring substantially similar confidentiality agreements. The modified text weakens these protections. By using 'should attempt' instead of 'shall', the obligation becomes less stringent. By using 'may need to know' and 'somewhat similar', the scope of permissible disclosure and the required similarity of the confidentiality agreements become vague. This ambiguity could weaken a claim under CUTSA, as it could be more difficult to prove that the disclosing party failed to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret when the NDA itself contains such ambiguity.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's protection of confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates disclosure only to those with a 'need to know' and with 'substantially similar' confidentiality agreements. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using 'should attempt', 'may need to know,' and 'somewhat similar'. According to the Law Explanation, this ambiguity weakens the ability to prove that the disclosing party took reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, potentially undermining a claim under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, supporting the law explanation. The modified text's ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce the confidentiality requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction because it fails to provide the level of protection that the original text aimed for and arguably required for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the other party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and each of the parties represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties agrees that it should try to treat all Confidential Information of the other party with a degree of care that it subjectively believes is similar to how it handles its own Confidential Information, and each of the parties represents that it may occasionally try to exercise what it subjectively perceives as reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications significantly weaken the obligation to protect confidential information. Changing 'shall treat' to 'should try to treat' weakens the commitment. Replacing 'same degree of care' with 'degree of care that it subjectively believes is similar' makes the standard subjective. Further weakening the party's own care for its own information through "may occasionally try to exercise what it subjectively perceives as reasonable care" creates very minimal protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text replaces the objective 'reasonable care' standard with a subjective 'what it subjectively perceives as reasonable care' standard. This weakens the obligation, potentially failing to meet the DTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. If a party only subjectively believes they are taking reasonable care, it may fall short of what a court would consider reasonable, making it harder to claim trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as implicitly suggested by the Delaware Trade Secrets Act, which defines 'misappropriation' in Snippet 1 as acquiring a trade secret through 'improper means,' including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By changing 'shall treat' to 'should try to treat' and making the standard subjective ('what it subjectively perceives as reasonable care'), the changed text undermines the legally expected standard of care for trade secrets. Snippet 2 emphasizes that Delaware codes may have newer versions, implicitly highlighting the importance of keeping up to date with the state's trade secret regulations.

perturbation.original_text: All materials (including, without limitation, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs and lists) furnished to one party by the other, and which are designated in writing to be the property of such party, shall remain the property of such party and shall be returned to it promptly at its request, together with any copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: All materials (including, without limitation, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs and lists) furnished to one party by the other, and which are designated in writing to possibly be the property of such party, should ideally remain the property of such party and should be returned to it when reasonably convenient at its request, possibly together with some copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original language dictates a clear obligation to return materials promptly. Modifying 'shall remain the property' to 'should ideally remain the property' weakens the property right. Changing 'shall be returned to it promptly' to 'should be returned to it when reasonably convenient' weakens the obligation to return the materials and introduces a subjective element of 'reasonable convenience.' Also, indicating the copies 'possibly' should be returned further weakens the protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP Rule 34 directly concerns discovery in litigation, the changes made to this contract section could indirectly hinder the discovery process and compliance with Rule 34. Rule 34 allows parties to request documents and tangible things. If the return of materials is delayed or incomplete because the obligation to return them is weakened (e.g., 'when reasonably convenient', 'possibly together with some copies thereof'), it could impede a party's ability to fully comply with a discovery request under Rule 34, potentially leading to sanctions or other adverse consequences. The changes introduce doubt and flexibility, potentially complicating and delaying the production of materials during discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes (a) In General. A party may serve on any other party a request within the scope of Rule 26(b) : (1) to produce and permit the requesting party or its representative to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items in the responding party's possession, custody, or control: (A) any designated documents or electronically stored information—including writings, drawings, graphs, charts, photographs, sound recordings, images, and other data or data compilations—stored in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or (B) any designated tangible things; or (2) to permit entry onto designated land or other property possessed or controlled by the responding party, so that the requesting party may inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property or any designated object or operation on it. (b) Procedure. (1) Contents of the Request. The request: (A) must describe with reasonable particularity each item or category of items to be inspected; (B) must specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts; and (C) may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (2) Responses and Objections. (A) Time to Respond. The party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served or — if the request was delivered under Rule 26(d)(2) — within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. A shorter or longer time may be s

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that could impede compliance with legal discovery obligations under FRCP Rule 34. The original text mandates a clear obligation to return materials promptly, but the modified text weakens this obligation by stating materials 'should ideally remain the property' and be returned 'when reasonably convenient,' and 'possibly together with some copies thereof.' This introduces subjective elements that could delay or prevent the full production of documents during discovery, potentially leading to sanctions for non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly outlines the scope of Rule 34, which includes producing documents and tangible things. The perturbation explanation indicates that the modifications weaken the obligation to return the materials, supporting a 'YES' determination because it could hinder compliance.
